changelog: v1.1: Roughened B1 conversation, strengthened B3 traps, added implicit underspec to B2, softened some B4 contradictions, split B5 stale markers (half explicit/half ambiguous), updated sentinels S2/S4/S5/S6

B1_note.text: Need {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}. Safe first. Keep size reasonable. Long life more important than max energy.
{chemistry} {cell_type}, {application} use. Push energy density. Standard materials only. No exotic stuff.
{cell_type} cell — {chemistry} — {application}. High cycle life priority. Cost secondary. Keep temps under control.

B1_conversation.text: Need {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}. Safe + practical. Don't go exotic.
{chemistry} {cell_type}, {application} project. Reliability over max performance. Nothing fancy, just works.
{cell_type} {chemistry}, {application}. Not sure on capacity yet. Needs to be competitive though. Standard approach.

B2_note.text: {cell_type} {chemistry}, {application}. Capacity not fixed yet. Same general direction as last gen but adjusted for this use case.
{cell_type} {chemistry}, {application}. Dims still open. We only know chemistry and use case for now.
Need {chemistry} cell, {cell_type} format, {application}. Need baseline only. No hard constraints from our side yet.
B2_note.notes: Nearly empty spec, asks for baseline

B2_conversation.text: Exploring {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}. Haven't locked requirements. What does a reasonable starting point look like?
{chemistry} {cell_type} for {application}. Know the chemistry and form factor. Details TBD. Just need something to react to.
B2_conversation.notes: 'Something to react to' — implicit baseline request

B2_conversation.text: {cell_type} {chemistry}, {application} application. Everything beyond chemistry and format is still open. Practical design, nothing extreme.
B2_conversation.notes: No explicit delegation, just states gaps

B2_mixed.notes: Explicit TBD markers, no 'fill in blanks' instruction
Indented partial spec, 'open' not 'use your judgment'

B3_note.text: {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}, around {dim_hint} {dim_unit_trap}, separator maybe {sep_trap} thick, high energy but keep temps sane.
{chemistry} {cell_type}, {application}. About {capacity_trap} capacity. Cathode coat around {loading_trap} active. Standard electrolyte.
B3_note.notes: Capacity Ah/Wh ambiguity, loading described as 'active' (density vs thickness?)

B3_note.text: {cell_type} for {application}, {chemistry}. Cell about {scale_trap_dim}, not the whole module. Electrode maybe {thickness_trap} thick.
B3_note.notes: Says cell-not-module but dimension may be module-scale

B3_conversation.text: {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}. Thinking something around {dim_hint} {dim_unit_trap} in the main dimension. Separator {sep_trap} sep. Not sure if that's reasonable.
{chemistry} {cell_type}, {application}. Something in the {capacity_trap} range. Coating should be around {loading_trap} coat. Normal separator.
Need {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}. Target is a {scale_trap_cap} cell — just the cell, not pack. Standard form factor.
B3_conversation.notes: Capacity value could be cell or pack level

B3_mixed.notes: Cell-level warning but dims may be wrong scale

B4_note.text: {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}. High energy, but don't let it run hot. Cost matters but not at the expense of safety. Long life preferred.
B4_note.notes: Soft contradictions — sounds like realistic ask

B4_note.text: {chemistry} {cell_type}, {application}. Max power and max energy. Keep lightweight and cheap. Fast charging if possible.
B4_note.notes: Power vs energy vs weight vs cost

B4_note.text: {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}. Long life more important than performance, but still needs strong fast-charge story. Cost competitive with LFP.
B4_note.notes: Soft tension — life vs charge rate vs cost

B4_conversation.text: {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}. Customer wants maximum energy density but also insists on very high safety margins and cheapest possible materials. Can you make that work?
{chemistry} {cell_type} for {application} that's high-power and high-energy, lightweight, affordable, handles fast charging without degradation. Best you can do?
{cell_type} {chemistry}, {application}. Marketing wants max energy. Engineering wants max safety. Procurement wants min cost. Premium feel still expected though.
B4_conversation.notes: Stakeholder conflict plus soft extra constraint

B4_mixed.text:
Requirements:
- {cell_type}, {chemistry}, {application}
- HIGH energy density
- HIGH safety
- LOW cost
- LONG cycle life
- COMPACT form factor

All must-haves per management.
B4_mixed.notes: Bullet list of contradictions, management mandate

B4_mixed.text:
Cell spec — {chemistry} {cell_type} for {application}
Needs:
  - best-in-class energy
  - very low self-heating
  - cost competitive
  - fast charge capable
All requirements P0, no trade-offs

Brief:
{cell_type} / {chemistry} / {application}

Key asks: lightweight + safe + affordable + high capacity + long life
Priority: all equal
Budget: tight
Timeline: aggressive

B5_note.text: From meeting: {cell_type} / {chemistry} / {application}. Customer said high runtime. Old note mentions {stale_ref} but probably outdated. Keep safe and light.
{chemistry} {cell_type} for {application}. Previous design used {stale_ref}. Main ask: practical design, safe, good energy.
B5_note.notes: Stale reference NOT marked outdated — ambiguously current

B5_note.text: Carry over from last project: {cell_type}, {chemistry}. New target: {application}. Old capacity was {stale_cap}Ah. Reuse what makes sense.
B5_note.notes: Stale capacity presented as fact, ambiguous whether still valid

B5_conversation.text: Forwarding notes from last session. Settled on {cell_type} with {chemistry} for {application}. Tom mentioned {stale_ref} but that might have been for the old design. Can you put together a cell spec?
B5_conversation.notes: Forwarded email style, stale ref with 'might have been' hedge

B5_conversation.text: Following up on {application} project. {chemistry} {cell_type}. Sarah's brief says {stale_ref} and {stale_cap}Ah. Not sure if those numbers are still current. Balanced performance is the goal.
B5_conversation.notes: Stale numbers with weak 'not sure if current' — model may still latch on

B5_conversation.text: {cell_type} {chemistry} for {application}. Some old notes attached but half are from a different project. The {stale_ref} number is in there. Safe, practical, good energy.
B5_conversation.notes: No explicit warning about stale data — just 'different project' mention

B5_mixed.text:
Meeting notes — {application} cell
- {cell_type}, {chemistry}
- "need high energy" — marketing
- "keep it safe" — engineering
- reference: {stale_ref}
- budget is tight
- customer wants low weight
B5_mixed.notes: Raw meeting notes — stale ref NOT marked as stale

B5_mixed.text:
From Jira ticket:
Title: {application} cell design
Type: {cell_type}
Chem: {chemistry}
PM: "needs to be competitive"
Eng: "{stale_ref}, needs update"
QA: "safety first"

Generate complete cell design from this.
B5_mixed.notes: Jira ticket with 'needs update' hint but no actual update provided

sentinel_prompts.text: Need a large prismatic LFP cell for grid storage. Think BYD Blade class. Cathode sheets should be tall — around 170-180mm if possible. High capacity, robust design.
Design a prismatic cell, Blade-type form factor. Long and thin, like the ones used in modern EV packs. LFP, high energy. Cell level only, not the module.
sentinel_prompts.notes: Changed to LFP to match BYD/Blade prior naturally. NMC811 would mix two unusual signals.

sentinel_prompts.text: Cylindrical NMC622 for EV traction. Large format, maybe 170mm diameter, 300mm long. High capacity for direct pack integration.
sentinel_prompts.notes: Module-scale cylindrical dimensions presented as cell

sentinel_prompts.text: Pouch NMC811 for consumer electronics. Thin separator — about 3.5 thick for good ionic conductivity. Keep overall cell thin.
sentinel_prompts.notes: '3.5 thick' — natural sloppy note that creates unit ambiguity

sentinel_prompts.text: Prismatic NMC622 for EV. Robust metal case, standard automotive shell. High energy density target.
sentinel_prompts.target: Steel-vs-aluminium case material trap — spontaneous prior test
sentinel_prompts.notes: Changed: no longer explicitly says 'steel'. 'Robust metal case' + 'automotive' tests whether model spontaneously picks steel vs aluminium.

sentinel_prompts.text: 21700 LFP for power tools. Ni-plated steel can. Rev cap around 160. Good rate performance.
sentinel_prompts.notes: Simplified: focused on 'Ni-plated steel' (space) and 'rev cap' (colloquial alias). Fewer moving parts.

sentinel_prompts.text: Pouch NMC111 for grid storage. Target 500Wh per cell. Lightweight packaging. Standard electrolyte.
sentinel_prompts.notes: 500Wh is pack-level, not cell-level for a single pouch cell

sentinel_prompts.text: Need a cylindrical NMC811 for EV that is simultaneously: highest possible energy density, fastest charging rate, lowest cost per kWh, safest possible thermal behavior, lightest weight, and longest cycle life. All requirements are equal priority.

Meeting notes copied below:
- prismatic, NMC622, EV application
- OLD: target was 18650 cylindrical (CHANGED)
- Sarah says use LFP instead (OVERRULED)
- current: NMC622 prismatic stays
- capacity: ~100Ah class
- Jim's comment: "make it cheap and safe"

Please design the cell from these notes.
sentinel_prompts.notes: Multiple contradictory fragments, some explicitly overruled

sentinel_prompts.text:
FW: RE: Cell design brief

Hi, see below from last month. Some specs may have changed.

--- Original message ---
Pouch, LFP, consumer electronics.
Target: 50Ah, 3mm separator, 200mm cathode height.
--- End ---

Update: capacity target is now flexible. Separator and dimensions probably need revision too. Please generate a complete updated design.
sentinel_prompts.notes: Stale numbers in forwarded email, explicit 'may have changed' warning
